request.question: Who owns the company that produced a CGI animated Christmas special in 1999 and has footage for its company logo taken from the short film "A Study in Wet?"

title: The Garfield Show
My Family (series 10)
The Little Troll Prince
Yogi Bear's All Star Comedy Christmas Caper
Olive, the Other Reindeer
The Curiosity Company
Grandma Got Run Over by a Reindeer (film)
Partly Cloudy
The Little Rascals Christmas Special
Descente
sentences: The Garfield Show is a French–American CGI animated television series.

Based on the American comic strip, "Garfield", the series is executive produced by Garfield creator, Jim Davis, and co-written and voice directed by Mark Evanier, who also wrote most of the episodes for the "Garfield and Friends" series.
Returning from "Garfield and Friends" are the voice actors Julie Payne (Liz) and Gregg Berger (Odie).
Frank Welker replaces Lorenzo Music (due to his death in 2001) as the voice of Garfield, and Wally Wingert replaces Thom Huge (due to his retirement that same year) as the voice of Jon Arbuckle.
Also returning is David Lander, reprising his role as Doc Boy from the earlier Garfield prime-time special "A Garfield Christmas Special" (1987).
The show is produced by Dargaud Media and Paws Inc.
The show is directed by Philippe Vidal and the music is composed by Laurent Bertaud and Jean-Christophe Prudhomme.
The tenth series of the BBC family sitcom "My Family" originally aired between 9 July 2010, and 27 August 2010, with a Christmas special that went to air on 24 December 2010.
The series was commissioned following consistent ratings from the previous series.
The opening episode, "Wheelie Ben", re-introduces the six main characters, with the addition of regular appearances from Kenzo Harper, played by Tayler Marshall.
However, the character of Roger Bailey only made an appearance in the series finale.
All episodes from the tenth series are thirty minutes in length, with the exception of the Christmas Special.
The series was once again produced by Rude Boy Productions, a company that produces comedies created by Fred Barron.
Unlike previous series of the show, which were filmed on a yearly basis, both Series 10 and 11 were filmed back-to-back.
For the first time in the show's history, two episodes of the series remained unaired for some time.
At the time of release, the DVD of the series contained two episodes that had yet to be broadcast on television.
On 17 June 2011, one of these two episodes were aired, and the other is scheduled to air on 22 July 2011.
The series averaged 4.55 million per episode; however, they managed to get over 6.00 million viewers for the Christmas Special.
The Little Troll Prince (onscreen title: The Little Troll Prince: A Christmas Parable) is a 1987 animated Christmas television special produced by Hanna-Barbera.
Backed by the International Lutheran Laymen's League, it has strong Christian themes of unconditional love, self-sacrifice, and redemption, with a substantial Protestant influence.
The story was a written parable script for the Christmas special with a lesson about the feeling of joy and love in one's heart, that can lead one from a cruel world to a light-hearted world.
Yogi Bear's All Star Comedy Christmas Caper is a 1982 animated Christmas television special starring Yogi Bear.
It is the third and final Yogi Christmas special.
Produced by Hanna-Barbera, it first aired on  21, 1982 (1982--) on CBS.
Olive, the Other Reindeer is a 1999 CGI animated Christmas television special written by Steve Young, and directed by Oscar Moore.
The feature was produced by Matt Groening's The Curiosity Company (best known for "Futurama"), and animated by DNA Productions.
It first aired on December 17, 1999 on Fox, produced by 20th Century Fox Television and Flower Films.
The special combines paperlike character art in 3-D environments.
Sometimes, traditional animation is used.
The Curiosity Company, Inc. is an American production company behind hit series "Futurama" and the 1999 television film "Olive, the Other Reindeer".
The company logo depicts the letter "C" being formed by a surfboard reflection against various ocean waves, alongside distorted water sounds.
The footage and sounds are taken from "A Study in Wet", a short film made by Matt's father, Homer.
Grandma Got Run Over by a Reindeer is a Canadian–American animated Christmas special, directed by Phil Roman.
The special first aired October 31, 2000 on The WB.
It has subsequently aired on The CW (the successor to The WB) and Cartoon Network during the Christmas season.
Since Warner Home Video distributed this special, Time Warner owns the copyrights.
The title and plot are based on the novelty song of the same name.
Partly Cloudy is a Pixar CGI animated short film written and directed by Peter Sohn and produced by Kevin Reher.
It was shown in theaters before Pixar's feature film "Up" and is a special feature on its DVD and Blu-ray release.
It was included in the Animation Show of Shows in 2009.
The Little Rascals Christmas Special is an animated Christmas television special based on the "Our Gang" comedies of the 1920s, 1930s, and early 1940s.
It first aired December 3, 1979 on NBC.

Descente Ltd. (株式会社デサント , Kabushiki-gaisha Desanto ) is a Japanese sports clothing and accessories company, first formed back in 1935, when Takeo Ishimoto started the company in Osaka as Ishimoto Shoten.
The company logo, over 50 years old, depicts 3 basic skiing techniques - traverse, schuss and side-slip.
Descente owns a portfolio of 16 in-house, purchased and licensed brands, among them Descente, Shiseist, Arena, Marmot and Srixon.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: Who owns the company that produced a CGI animated Christmas special in 1999 and has footage for its company logo taken from the short film "A Study in Wet?"
hop1_docs: The Curiosity Company | The Curiosity Company, Inc. is an American production company behind hit series "Futurama" and the 1999 television film "Olive, the Other Reindeer".  The company logo depicts the letter "C" being formed by a surfboard reflection against various ocean waves, alongside distorted water sounds.  The footage and sounds are taken from "A Study in Wet", a short film made by Matt's father, Homer.

Olive, the Other Reindeer | Olive, the Other Reindeer is a 1999 CGI animated Christmas television special written by Steve Young, and directed by Oscar Moore.  The feature was produced by Matt Groening's The Curiosity Company (best known for "Futurama"), and animated by DNA Productions.  It first aired on December 17, 1999 on Fox, produced by 20th Century Fox Television and Flower Films.  The special combines paperlike character art in 3-D environments.  Sometimes, traditional animation is used.

Act3animation | Act3animation (Act3) is an Australian CGI company, specializing in character animation, based in Melbourne, Australia.  Founded in 1992 by current creative director, Mike Hollands, Act3 has produced various TVCs, FMVs and short films.

Fatkat | FatKat animation was an animation studio located in Miramichi, New Brunswick.  The company was formed from animators who left Helix Animation in 1999.  It was owned and operated by Gene Fowler and his employees.  This company was started in Halifax, Nova Scotia and was taken over by Trainingscape which saw the creation of over 40 training films for various companies.

Oxford Scientific Films | Oxford Scientific Films (OSF) is a British company that produces natural history and documentary programmes.  Founded on 8 July 1968, by noted documentary filmmaker Gerald Thompson, the independent film company broke new ground in the world of documentaries, using new filming techniques and capturing footage of never before filmed activities of its various subjects.  In 1996, Oxford Scientific Films was sold to Circle Communications, where it retained its own identity as a division within the company.  The following year, Circle Communications was taken over by Southern Star Entertainment UK.  Under the new ownership, Oxford Scientific Films continued to enhance its reputation for innovative film-making, producing multiple award-winning series and films, including the highly acclaimed Animal Planet series, "Meerkat Manor".

Sanzigen | Sanzigen Inc. (株式会社サンジゲン , Kabushiki gaisha Sanjigen ) is a Japanese animation company specializing in CGI animation.  The studio was founded in 2003, later becoming formally incorporated in 2006 by former employees of Gonzo, and has contributed to various anime series and films.  Its name is derived from the Japanese word for "three-dimensional" (三次元 , sanjigen ) .

Happy Trails Animation | Happy Trails Animation LLC, is an American animation studio specializing in feature films, commercials, music videos, broadcast graphics and short films.  It is owned by Andy and Amy Collen and is located in Portland, Oregon.  The company has two divisions, one for digital content and the other for commercial work such as advertisements and music or corporate videos.  Happy Trails Animation has received 11 Telly Awards, 1 Crystal Heart Award, 3 Davey Awards, and numerous international film festival acknowledgments from its short films over the past 18 years.
response.summary_1: Matt Groening — owner of The Curiosity Company (Olive, the Other Reindeer; The Curiosity Company).
hop2_docs: The Curiosity Company | The Curiosity Company, Inc. is an American production company behind hit series "Futurama" and the 1999 television film "Olive, the Other Reindeer".  The company logo depicts the letter "C" being formed by a surfboard reflection against various ocean waves, alongside distorted water sounds.  The footage and sounds are taken from "A Study in Wet", a short film made by Matt's father, Homer.

Curiosity (learning platform) | Curiosity was founded by CEO Gabe Vehovsky in November 2014, after being launched as part of Discovery Communications in January 2014.  Vehovsky, a Discovery executive responsible for seeking business opportunities in online education, recommended that the company build a platform aimed at providing educational content.  Curiosity spun off from Discovery in November 2014.  Curiosity raised $6 million in a Series A funding round in November 2014 from a number of Chicago-based investors, including Pritzker Group Venture Capital, Origin Ventures, Chicago Ventures, and Corazon Capital.

The Old Curiosity Shop (1914 film) | The Old Curiosity Shop is a 1914 British silent drama film directed by Thomas Bentley and starring Mai Deacon, Warwick Buckland and Alma Taylor.  It was based on the 1840 novel "The Old Curiosity Shop" by Charles Dickens, and was the first of three film adaptations of the story by Bentley.  It was made by the Hepworth Company, the leading British film studio before the First World War.

Cinar Corp v Robinson | In 1982, Claude Robinson, an artist, drew the first sketches of the characters for a proposed children’s television series to be called "The Adventures of Robinson Curiosity" (French: "Les aventures de Robinson Curiosité" ), based in part on the novel "Robinson Crusoe", but also on his own life experiences.  In 1985, a certificate of registration was issued under the "Copyright Act" for "Robinson Curiosity", listing Robinson as author and Les Productions Nilem inc., a corporation of which he was the sole shareholder, as the owner of the work.

Chacon (1912) | Chacon is a 72 ft dry docked wooden vessel and roadside curiosity in Chugiak, Alaska, United States.  The former fishing vessel currently serves as a memorial for its most recent owner, Thillman Wallace of Chugiak (1932 – 2015).

The Old Curiosity Shop (1911 film) | The Old Curiosity Shop is a 1911 American silent short drama film produced by the Thanhouser Company.  The film is an adaptation of "The Old Curiosity Shop" by Charles Dickens that was limited to the time constrictions of the single reel format.  The film focuses on the grandfather who gambles into poverty and the consequences which eventually claim the life of Little Nell.  Its survival and attribution as a Thanhouser film was noted by Kamilla Elliott in her 2003 book "Rethinking the Novel/Film Debate" under the title "Little Nell".  In 2012, the work was confirmed to be a Thanhouser production at the Pordenone Silent Film Festival.  The identification of the film as "Little Nell" arose due to head of the film having been lost.

The Curiosity Club | The Curiosity Club is a curricula enrichment programme owned by Curiosity Learning Limited.  It was founded in 2015 by John Gordon-Reid and Christopher Lloyd (world history author) alongside the “learning through choice” concept.  Curiosity Learning Limited is based in Kent and operates nationwide and in North America.
response.summary_2: Matt Groening. The Curiosity Company produced the 1999 TV film "Olive, the Other Reindeer," and its logo uses footage and sounds taken from the short film "A Study in Wet" by Groening's father Homer (passage [1]).
